content.Title: Management of Patients With Peripheral Artery Disease (Compilation of 2005 and 2011 ACCF/AHA Guideline Recommendations)
Authors/Contributors: Jeffrey L. Anderson, MD, FACC, FAHA
Jonathan L. Halperin, MD, FACC, FAHA
Biykem Bozkurt, MD, PhD, FACC, FAHA
Ralph G. Brindis, MD, MPH, MACC
Judith S. Hochman, MD, FACC, FAHA
Richard J. Kovacs, MD, FACC, FAHA
Susan J. Pressler, PhD, RN, FAAN, FAHA
Frank W. Sellke, MD, FACC, FAHA

Management of patients with peripheral artery disease: a report of the American College of Cardiology Foundation/American Heart Association Task Force on Practice Guidelines. J Am Coll Cardiol 2013;61:1555–70. http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jacc.2013.01.004.

sections.title: Vascular History and Physical Examination: Recommendations

details: Individuals at risk for lower extremity peripheral artery disease (PAD) should undergo a vascular review of symptoms for assessing walking impairment, claudication, ischemic rest pain, and/or the presence of nonhealing wounds.
Comprehensive pulse examination and inspection of the feet are recommended for individuals at risk for lower extremity PAD.
Individuals over 50 years of age should be asked about a family history of a first-order relative with an abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA).

subsections.title: Clinical Presentation
details: A history of walking impairment, claudication, ischemic rest pain, and/or nonhealing wounds is required for adults 50 years and older with atherosclerosis risk factors and for adults 70 years and older.
Asymptomatic lower extremity PAD should be identified by measuring the ankle-brachial index (ABI) to reduce myocardial infarction (MI), stroke, and death risks.
Smoking cessation, lipid lowering, and diabetes and hypertension treatments are recommended for individuals with asymptomatic lower extremity PAD.
Antiplatelet therapy is indicated to reduce the risk of adverse cardiovascular ischemic events.

subsections.title: Asymptomatic
details: Exercise ABI measurement is useful for diagnosing PAD in individuals with resting ABI of 0.91 to 1.30 with claudication symptoms.
Toe-brachial index or pulse volume recording measurement can enhance PAD diagnosis in individuals with ABI > 1.30 and other clinical evidence of atherosclerosis.

Class IIb.1: Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibition may be considered for individuals with asymptomatic lower extremity PAD for cardiovascular risk reduction.

2.1.2 Claudication.1: Patients with symptoms of intermittent claudication should undergo a vascular physical examination, including measurement of the ABI.

2.1.2 Claudication.2: In patients with symptoms of intermittent claudication, the ABI should be measured after exercise if the resting index is normal.

2.1.2 Claudication.3: Patients with intermittent claudication should have significant functional impairment with a reasonable likelihood of symptomatic improvement and absence of other disease that would comparably limit exercise even if claudication was improved before undergoing an evaluation for revascularization.

2.1.4 Acute Limb Ischemia.1: Patients with acute limb ischemia and a salvageable extremity should undergo emergent evaluation that defines the anatomic level of occlusion and that leads to prompt endovascular or surgical revascularization.

2.2.1 Updated Recommendation.1: The resting ABI should be used to establish the lower extremity PAD diagnosis in patients with suspected lower extremity PAD, defined as individuals with 1 or more of the following: exertional leg symptoms, nonhealing wounds, age 65 and older, or 50 years and older with a history of smoking or diabetes.

2.2.1 Updated Recommendation.2: The ABI should be measured in both legs in all new patients with PAD of any severity to confirm the diagnosis of lower extremity PAD and establish a baseline.

2.2.1 Updated Recommendation.3: The toe-brachial index should be used to establish the lower extremity PAD diagnosis in patients in whom lower extremity

2.1.5 Prior Limb Arterial Revascularizat.1: Long-term patency of infraininguinal bypass grafts should be evaluated in a surveillance program, which should include an interval vascular history, resting ABIs, physical examination, and a duplex ultrasound at regular intervals if a venous conduit has been used.

2.2.1 Ankle- and Toe-Brachial Indices, S.1: The resting ABI should be used to establish the lower extremity PAD diagnosis in patients with suspected lower extremity PAD, defined as individuals with 1 or more of the following: exertional leg symptoms, nonhealing wounds, age 65 and older, or 50 years and older with a history of smoking or diabetes.

topics.classIia: Pulse volume recordings are reasonable to establish the initial lower extremity PAD diagnosis, assess localization and severity, and follow the status of lower extremity revascularization procedures.

topics.classI: Continuous-wave Doppler ultrasound blood flow measurements are useful to provide an accurate assessment of lower extremity PAD location and severity, to follow lower extremity PAD progression, and to provide quantitative follow-up after revascularization procedures.

topics.section: 2.2.4 Treadmill Exercise Testing With and Without ABI Assessments and 6-Minute Walk Test
classI: Exercise treadmill tests are recommended to provide the most objective evidence of the magnitude of the functional limitation of claudication and to measure the response to therapy.
A standardized exercise protocol should be used to ensure reproducibility of measurements of pain-free walking distance and maximal walking distance.
Exercise treadmill tests with measurement of pre-exercise and postexercise ABI values are recommended for differentiating arterial claudication from nonarterial claudication.
classIib: A 6-minute walk test may be reasonable to provide an objective assessment of claudication and response to therapy in elderly individuals or others not amenable to treadmill testing.

classI: Duplex ultrasound of the extremities is useful to diagnose anatomic location and degree of stenosis of PAD.
Duplex ultrasound is recommended for routine surveillance after bypass with a venous conduit.
classIia: Duplex ultrasound of the extremities can be useful to select patients as candidates for endovascular intervention.
Duplex ultrasound can be useful to select patients as candidates for surgical bypass and to select the sites of surgical anastomosis.
topics.classIib: The use of duplex ultrasound is not well established to assess long-term patency of percutaneous transluminal angioplasty.

classIib: Computed tomographic angiography (CTA) of the extremities may be considered to diagnose anatomic location and presence of significant stenosis in patients with lower extremity PAD.
CTA of the extremities may be considered as a substitute for magnetic resonance angiography for those with contraindications to MRA.

classI: MRA of the extremities is useful to diagnose anatomic location and degree of stenosis of PAD.
MRA of the extremities should be performed with gadolinium enhancement.
MRA of the extremities is useful in selecting patients with lower extremity PAD as candidates for endovascular intervention.
classIib: MRA of the extremities may be considered to select patients with lower extremity PAD as candidates for surgical bypass and to select the sites of surgical anastomosis.
MRA of the extremities may be considered for postrevascularization surveillance.

topics.classI: Decisions regarding the potential utility of invasive therapy should be made with a complete anatomic assessment.

content.assessment: Assessment of the affected arterial territory includes imaging and angiography techniques (Level of Evidence: B). Digital subtraction angiography is recommended (Level of Evidence: A).
content.angiography: Complete evaluation before contrast angiography is necessary (Level of Evidence: C). Selective catheter placement is recommended (Level of Evidence: C).
content.diagnostic imaging: Lower extremity arteriogram imaging should be conducted with specific guidelines (Level of Evidence: B).

treatment.lipid-lowering drugs: Hydroxymethyl glutaryl coenzyme-A reductase inhibitor (statin) for patients to lower cholesterol (Level of Evidence: B).
treatment.antihypertensive drugs: Antihypertensive therapy for hypertension with PAD (Class I, Level of Evidence: A).
treatment.diabetes therapies: Proper foot care and diabetes treatment (Class I, Level of Evidence: B).
treatment.smoking cessation: Patients should be advised to stop tobacco use (Class I, Level of Evidence: A).

Homocysteine-lowering Drugs.Class IIB: The effectiveness of folic acid and B12 vitamin supplements in individuals with lower extremity PAD and homocysteine levels greater than 14 micromoles per liter is not well established. (Level of Evidence: C)

Class I: Antiplatelet therapy is recommended to reduce MI, stroke, and vascular death risk in those with symptomatic atherosclerotic lower extremity PAD including those with intermittent claudication or CLI, prior lower extremity revascularization, or amputation. (Level of Evidence: A)
Aspirin (75 to 325 mg daily) is recommended to reduce MI, stroke, or vascular death risk in symptomatic atherosclerotic lower extremity PAD. (Level of Evidence: B)
Clopidogrel (75 mg per day) is recommended as an alternative to aspirin. (Level of Evidence: B)
Antiplatelet and Antithrombotic Drugs.Class IIA: Antiplatelet therapy can be useful to reduce the risk of MI, stroke, or vascular death in asymptomatic individuals with an ABI less than or equal to 0.90. (Level of Evidence: C)
Class IIB: The usefulness of antiplatelet therapy in asymptomatic individuals with borderline ABI is not well established. (Level of Evidence: B)
The combination of aspirin and clopidogrel may be considered in symptomatic individuals not at increased risk of bleeding and who are at high perceived cardiovascular risk.
Antiplatelet and Antithrombotic Drugs.Class III: Warfarin addition to antiplatelet therapy in individuals with atherosclerotic lower extremity PAD is of no benefit and potentially harmful. (Level of Evidence: B)

Class I: Supervised exercise training is recommended for intermittent claudication. (Level of Evidence: A)
Exercise should be performed for 30 to 45 minutes at least 3 times a week for 12 weeks.
Exercise and Lower Extremity PAD Rehabil.Class IIB: The effectiveness of unsupervised exercise programs is not well established. (Level of Evidence: B)

Class I: Cilostazol (100 mg orally 2 times per day) improves symptoms and increases walking distance in intermittent claudication. (Level of Evidence: A)
A therapeutic trial of cilostazol should be considered in all patients with lifestyle-limiting claudication.
Class IIB: Pentoxifylline (400 mg 3 times per day) may be considered as a second-line alternative to cilostazol. (Level of Evidence: A)
The effectiveness of pentoxifylline is marginal and not well established. (Level of Evidence: C)
The efficacy of L-arginine, propionyl-L-carnitine, and ginkgo biloba in claudication is not well established. (Level of Evidence: B)
Class III: Vasodilator prostaglandins (e.g., beraprost, iloprost) and vitamin E are not effective.
Chelation therapy is not indicated for claudication treatment.

Endovascular Treatment.Class I: Endovascular procedures are indicated for intermittent claudication with vocational or lifestyle-limiting disability.

1. Indications for Surgical Intervention.Class I: Revascularization is indicated for symptomatic improvement in patients with significant disability and an inadequate response to other therapies. Endovascular intervention is recommended for TASC Type A iliac and femoropopliteal lesions.

Class IIa.Endovascular Techniques: Stents, cutting balloons, atherectomy devices, and thermal devices can be used as salvage therapy for femoral, popliteal, and tibial arteries.
Class IIa.Stenting: Recommended as primary therapy for common iliac artery stenosis/occlusions and external iliac artery stenosis/occlusions.

Class IIb.Effectiveness: The effectiveness of stents and other devices for femoral-popliteal arterial lesions is not well-established.

Class III.Not Indicated: Endovascular intervention is not indicated without significant pressure gradient and not as prophylactic therapy in asymptomatic PAD.

Preoperative Evaluation.Class I: Risk evaluation is necessary in patients with PAD undergoing major surgical intervention.

Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease.Class I: Aortobifemoral bypass and iliac endarterectomy/iliobifemoral bypass are recommended surgical treatments.
Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease.Class IIb: Axillofemoral-femoral bypass can be considered in limited settings.
Aortoiliac Occlusive Disease.Class III: Axillofemoral-femoral bypass should not be used routinely.

Infrainguinal Disease.Class I: Autogenous vein bypasses to the popliteal artery are recommended above or below the knee.
Infrainguinal Disease.Class IIa: Synthetic grafts to the popliteal artery below the knee are reasonable in certain cases.
Infrainguinal Disease.Class IIb: Femoral-tibial artery bypass with autogenous vein is considered in rare cases.

Level of Evidence Definitions.Class IIa: Weight of evidence/opinion is in favor of usefulness/efficacy.
Level of Evidence Definitions.Class IIb: Usefulness/efficacy is less well established by evidence/opinion.
Level of Evidence Definitions.Class III: Interventions are not useful/effective or may be harmful.

Class I: Patients with autogenous vein conduits should be monitored for claudication symptoms, ABIs, and graft examination.
Periodic evaluations for patients with femoral-popliteal or lower extremity bypass.

Class III: Pentoxifylline is not useful for CLI.

Class I: Inflow lesions should be treated in patients with combined inflow and outflow disease.
Treatment of inflow lesions is emphasized.
Amputation for significant necrosis or uncorrectable contracture.
Class III: Surgical intervention not indicated if inflow not severely decreased.

Class I: Catheter-based thrombolysis is effective for acute limb ischemia.
Class IIa: Mechanical thrombectomy devices can aid thrombolysis.
Class IIb: Consider thrombolysis for CLI more than 14 days old.

class I: Bypasses to the above-knee popliteal artery should be constructed with autogenous saphenous vein when possible. (Level of Evidence: A)
Bypasses to the below-knee popliteal artery should be constructed with autogenous vein when possible. (Level of Evidence: A)
The most distal artery with continuous flow from above and without a stenosis greater than 20% should be used as the point of origin for a distal bypass. (Level of Evidence: B)
The tibial or pedal artery that is capable of providing continuous and uncompromised outflow to the foot should be used as the site of distal anastomosis. (Level of Evidence: B)

Femoral-tibial artery bypasses should be constructed with autogenous vein, including the ipsilateral greater saphenous vein, or if unavailable, other sources of vein from the leg or arm. (Level of Evidence: B)

Composite sequential femoropopliteal-tibial bypass and bypass to an isolated popliteal arterial segment that has collateral outflow to the foot are both acceptable methods of revascularization and should be considered when no other form of bypass with adequate autogenous conduit is possible. (Level of Evidence: B)

If no autogenous vein is available, a prosthetic femoral-tibial bypass, and possibly an adjunctive procedure, such as arteriovenous fistula or vein interposition or cuff, should be used when amputation is imminent. (Level of Evidence: B)
class IIa: Prosthetic material can be used effectively for bypasses to the below-knee popliteal artery when no autogenous vein from ipsilateral or contralateral leg or arms is available. (Level of Evidence: B)

class I: Unless contraindicated, all patients undergoing revascularization for CLI should be placed on antiplatelet therapy, and this treatment should be continued indefinitely. (Level of Evidence: A)

Patients who have undergone placement of aortobifemoral bypass grafts should be followed up with periodic evaluations that record any return or progression of ischemic symptoms, the presence of femoral pulses, and ABIs. (Level of Evidence: B)

If infection, ischemic ulcers, or gangrenous lesions persist and the ABI is less than 0.8 after correction of inflow, an outflow procedure should be performed that bypasses all major distal stenoses and occlusions. (Level of Evidence: A)

Patients who have undergone placement of a lower extremity bypass with autogenous vein should undergo for at least 2 years periodic examinations that record any return or progression of ischemic symptoms; a physical examination, with concentration on pulse examination of the proximal, graft, and outflow vessels; and duplex imaging of the entire length of the graft, with measurement of peak systolic velocities and calculation of velocity ratios across all lesions. (Level of Evidence: A)

Patients who have undergone placement of a synthetic lower extremity bypass graft should undergo periodic examinations that record any return of ischemic symptoms; a pulse examination of the proximal, graft, and outflow vessels; and assessment of ABIs at rest and after exercise for at least 2 years after implantation. (Level of Evidence: A)

The performance of diagnostic studies to identify clinically significant renal artery stenosis (RAS) is indicated in patients with the onset of hypertension before the age of 30 years. (Level of Evidence: B)

The performance of diagnostic studies to identify clinically significant RAS is indicated in patients with the onset of severe hypertension (as defined in the Seventh Report of the Joint National Committee on Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Treatment of High Blood Pressure: the JNC-7 report) after the age of 55 years. (Level of Evidence: B)

The performance of diagnostic studies to identify clinically significant RAS is indicated in patients with the following characteristics: (a) accelerated hypertension (sudden and persistent worsening of previously controlled hypertension); (b) resistant hypertension (defined as the failure to achieve goal blood pressure in patients who are adhering to full doses of an appropriate 3-drug regimen that includes a diuretic); or (c) malignant hypertension (hypertension with coexistent evidence of acute end-organ damage, i.e., acute renal failure, acutely decompensated congestive heart failure, new visual or neurological disturbance, and/or advanced [grade III to IV] retinopathy). (Level of Evidence: C)

The performance of diagnostic studies to identify clinically significant RAS is indicated in patients with new azotemia or worsening renal function after the administration of an ACE inhibitor or an angiotensin receptor blocking agent. (Level of Evidence: B)

The performance of diagnostic studies to identify clinically significant RAS is indicated in patients with an unexplained atrophic kidney or a discrepancy in size between the 2 kidneys of greater than 1.5 cm. (Level of Evidence: B)
The performance of diagnostic studies to identify clinically significant RAS is indicated in patients with sudden, unexplained pulmonary edema (especially azotemic patients). (Level of Evidence: B)
class IIa: The performance of diagnostic studies to identify clinically significant RAS is reasonable in patients with unexplained renal failure, including individuals starting renal replacement therapy (dialysis or renal transplantation). (Level of Evidence: B)
class III: The performance of arteriography to identify significant RAS may be reasonable in patients with multivessel coronary artery disease.

content.source: JACC Vol. 61, No. 14, 2013 - Management of Patients With PAD

Class I: Duplex ultrasonography recommended for RAS diagnosis.
CTA recommended for RAS diagnosis in individuals with normal renal function.
MRA recommended for RAS diagnosis.
Catheter angiography recommended if noninvasive tests are inconclusive.
Class III: Selective renal vein renin measurements not recommended.

Class I: ACE inhibitors, Angiotensin receptor blockers, Calcium-channel blockers, and Beta blockers are effective for hypertension treatment with unilateral RAS.

Hypertension: Reasonable for patients with significant RAS and hypertension.
Preservation of renal function may be considered.
Impact of RAS on Congestive Heart Failur: Indicated for patients with significant RAS.

Class I: Renal stent placement for ostial atherosclerotic RAS lesions.
Balloon angioplasty with stent placement if necessary.
Vascular surgical reconstruction for patients with fibromuscular dysplastic RAS.
Reconstruction indicated for patients with atherosclerotic RAS with certain clinical indications.

subsections.title: Acute Intestinal Ischemia Caused by Arterial Obstruction

class1: Patients with acute abdominal pain out of proportion to physical findings and with a history of cardiovascular disease should be suspected of having acute intestinal ischemia.
Patients who develop acute abdominal pain after arterial interventions involving major arteries or with certain cardiac conditions should be suspected of having acute intestinal ischemia.
class3: Duplex sonography of the abdomen is not appropriate for suspected acute intestinal ischemia.

class1: Surgical treatment involves bowel resection and 'second look' operations after revascularization.

class2b: Percutaneous interventions are appropriate for selected patients but may still require laparotomy.

class1: Nonocclusive ischemia should be suspected in patients with low blood flow or shock, patients using vasoconstrictor substances, and those undergoing abdominal surgery.
Arteriography is indicated in suspected nonocclusive ischemia not responding to treatment.
Immediate treatment of underlying shock state is crucial.
Laparotomy and bowel resection is indicated for patients with persistent symptoms.
class2a: Transcatheter administration of vasodilators is indicated for specific cases of nonocclusive ischemia.

subsections.title: Diagnosis
class1: Chronic intestinal ischemia should be suspected with abdominal pain and weight loss, especially with cardiovascular disease.
Duplex ultrasound, CTA, and MRA are useful tests.
Diagnostic angiography should be obtained if noninvasive testing is unavailable.

subsections.title: Endovascular Treatment for Chronic Intestinal Ischemia
class1: Percutaneous endovascular treatment is indicated for chronic intestinal ischemia.

subsections.title: Surgical Treatment
class1: Surgical treatment is indicated for chronic intestinal ischemia.
class2b: Revascularization may be considered for asymptomatic obstructions in specific cases.
class3: Surgical revascularization is not indicated for patients with asymptomatic obstructions unless undergoing surgery for other conditions.

content.Section: 5. Aneurysms of the Abdominal Aorta, Its Branch Vessels, and the Lower Extremities: Recommendations

Content.Title: 5.1.1. Etiology
Recommendations: Class I: Blood pressure and fasting serum lipid values should be monitored and controlled in patients with AAAs and atherosclerotic disease. Smoking cessation should be advised for patients with aneurysms or a family history of aneurysms.

Content.Title: 5.1.2. Natural History
Recommendations: Class I: Patients with infrarenal or juxtarenal AAAs ≥5.5 cm should undergo repair to eliminate rupture risk.
Class Ia: Repair can be beneficial in patients with AAAs 5.0 - 5.4 cm.
Class IIa: Repair may be beneficial for patients with type IV thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysms larger than 5.5 to 6.0 cm.

Content.Title: 5.1.3. Diagnosis
Recommendations: Class I: Clinical suspicion necessitates evaluation. Repair indicated for symptomatic aortic aneurysms regardless of diameter.

Content.Title: 5.1.4. Observational Management
Recommendations: Class I: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents are indicated for patients with coronary artery disease undergoing repair.
Class IIb: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may reduce aneurysm expansion rate.

Content.Title: 5.1.5. Prevention of Aortic Aneurysm Rupture
Recommendations: Class I: Updated recommendations for repair of infrarenal AAAs or iliac aneurysms in suitable patients.

Subsections.Title: 5.2. Visceral Artery Aneurysms
Content: Class I: Intervention indicated for aneurysms ≥2.0 cm in diameter.
Class IIa: Intervention probably indicated for aneurysms ≥2.0 cm in younger women considering pregnancy.

Subsections.Title: 5.3. Lower Extremity Aneurysms
Content: Class I: Imaging is recommended for patients with femoral or popliteal aneurysms.

class I.recommendation: Patients with a palpable popliteal mass should undergo an ultrasound examination to exclude popliteal aneurysm.
Patients with popliteal aneurysms 2.0 cm in diameter or larger should undergo repair to reduce the risk of thromboembolic complications and limb loss.
Patients with anastomotic pseudoaneurysms or symptomatic femoral artery aneurysms should undergo repair.

class IIa.recommendation: Surveillance by annual ultrasound imaging is suggested for patients with asymptomatic femoral artery true aneurysms smaller than 3.0 cm in diameter.

In patients with acute ischemia and popliteal artery aneurysms and absent runoff, catheter-directed thrombolysis or mechanical thrombectomy (or both) is suggested to restore distal runoff and resolve emboli.

class IIb.recommendation: In patients with asymptomatic enlargement of the popliteal arteries twice the normal diameter for age and gender, annual ultrasound monitoring is reasonable.
In patients with femoral or popliteal artery aneurysms, administration of antiplatelet medication may be beneficial.

class I.recommendation: Patients with suspected femoral pseudoaneurysms should be evaluated by duplex ultrasonography.
Initial treatment with ultrasound-guided compression or thrombin injection is recommended in patients with large and/or symptomatic femoral artery pseudoaneurysms.

class IIa.recommendation: Surgical repair is reasonable in patients with femoral artery pseudoaneurysms 2.0 cm in diameter or larger that persist or recur after ultrasound-guided compression or thrombin injection.
Reevaluation by ultrasound 1 month after the original injury can be useful in patients with asymptomatic femoral artery pseudoaneurysms smaller than 2.0 cm in diameter.
